# MLmetrics: Machine Learning for Econometricians {.unnumbered}

This open textbook introduces modern machine-learning methods for graduate students in econometrics. The aim is not to replace econometric reasoning with black-box prediction, but to show how flexible ML tools can be used carefully in the settings econometricians actually face: forecasting, financial risk, macroeconomic data, firm-level panels, nonlinear dependence, and distributional uncertainty.

The book assumes a strong background in econometrics and statistics, but no prior exposure to machine learning. Concepts such as cross-validation, neural networks, tree-based methods, boosting, conformal prediction, and foundation models for economic text are introduced from first principles and connected to familiar econometric ideas such as likelihood, loss functions, forecast evaluation, time-series dependence, and model selection.

## Why Does This Book Exist? {.unnumbered}

This book grew out of two related motivations. The first was my own curiosity about how modern machine-learning methods should be adapted to the econometric problems that arise in research on forecasting, macro-finance, and predictive distributions. The second was teaching the MSc course Machine Learning at the Econometrics Institute at Erasmus University Rotterdam. In that course, I kept running into the same problem: standard ML texts are usually written for a different audience. They often assume little prior statistical training, work mostly with independent and identically distributed data, and motivate ideas with image or text classification. The econometric issues that arise naturally in economic and financial applications - serial dependence, distributional forecasting, real-time information sets, and leakage from invalid validation designs - usually receive much less attention.

At the same time, econometrics texts that discuss prediction or regularization rarely connect those topics to the broader ML toolkit, and they seldom explain how methods such as neural networks or gradient boosting should be adapted when the data have the structure of a macroeconomic or financial time series.

The aim of these notes is to bridge that gap for students with a solid econometrics background. They are not a substitute for the many strong ML resources that already exist. Instead, they frame modern machine learning in a language that econometricians can use immediately.

:::{.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
## Who This Book Is For

This book is written for MSc students, research master's students, and early PhD students in econometrics, economics, finance, and related quantitative fields.

Readers should be comfortable with probability, mathematical statistics, regression, likelihood-based estimation, and basic time-series methods. No prior knowledge of neural networks, random forests, boosting, or conformal prediction is assumed.
:::

## What You Will Learn {.unnumbered}

Across the book, the emphasis is on the parts of machine learning that matter most for econometric work:

- how to evaluate predictions when dependence, nonstationarity, and information sets matter
- how to use flexible models without contaminating inference through leakage or invalid validation schemes
- how to think about predictive distributions, scoring rules, tail risk, and uncertainty quantification
- how neural networks and tree-based methods behave in economic and financial applications
- how to tune, compare, and diagnose models in a way that remains statistically defensible

## What Makes This Book Econometric? {.unnumbered}

Machine-learning examples are often presented in clean i.i.d. settings. Econometric applications rarely look like that. This book repeatedly emphasizes:

- time-series dependence and real-time forecast evaluation
- heteroskedasticity, tail risk, and predictive distributions
- leakage from inappropriate validation schemes
- nonlinearities and interactions in economic data

The guiding question throughout is simple: how can we use flexible machine-learning methods while respecting the information sets, dependence structures, and evaluation problems that arise in econometric work?

## Why Python Here? {.unnumbered}

Python is now a standard part of the machine-learning toolkit in academia and industry, and it has a mature ecosystem for numerical computation, model training, and scientific visualization. This book uses Python because it makes the implementation of modern ML methods accessible without changing the statistical questions. The focus remains on model choice, loss functions, validation design, and forecast evaluation rather than on software for its own sake.

## What This Book Does Not Cover {.unnumbered}

This book is not a general introduction to econometrics, and it is not a full treatment of time-series econometrics. It does not replace a dedicated treatment of ARIMA, GARCH, state-space models, causal inference, or asymptotic theory. Those topics are assumed as background when needed.

It is also not a software-engineering manual. The code examples are there to make the statistical ideas concrete, not to provide production-ready ML pipelines.

## Book Roadmap {.unnumbered}

The chapters are organized into four parts.

| Part | Chapters | Focus |
|------|----------|-------|
| Background | [Information Theory](information_theory.qmd), [Cross Validation](cross_validation.qmd), [Evaluating Predictive Distributions](evaluating_distributions.qmd), [Optimization](optimization.qmd) | Core tools for measuring uncertainty, selecting models, evaluating forecasts, and training flexible models. |
| Neural Networks | [Feed-Forward Networks](feed_forward_nns.qmd), [Recurrent Networks](recurrent_neural_networks.qmd), [LSTM Networks](lstm_neural_networks.qmd), [Empirical Time-Series Exercise](nn_example.qmd), [Distribution Modeling](distributions_via_nns.qmd) | Neural-network models for nonlinear prediction, sequential data, and distributional forecasting. |
| Tree-Based Methods | [Decision Trees](decision_trees.qmd), [Random Forests](random_forests.qmd), [Gradient Boosting](gradient_boosting.qmd), [Advanced Tree-Based Methods](adv_tree_based_methods.qmd) | Interpretable trees, variance reduction by forests, stagewise boosting, and distributional tree methods. |
| Further Topics | [Hyperparameter Optimization](hpo.qmd), [Conformal Prediction](conformal_prediction.qmd), [Foundation Models for Economic Text](foundation_models.qmd), [Data Sets Used in This Book](datasets.qmd) | Modern tools for tuning, uncertainty quantification, text as an econometric input, and the data sources that anchor the empirical examples. |

## How To Use This Book {.unnumbered}

Each chapter combines formal notation, econometric interpretation, and Python examples. The code chunks are there to make the ideas concrete, but the mathematical arguments are written so the central logic can be followed without running code.

The exercises are designed with handwritten exam preparation in mind. They emphasize derivations, forecast-evaluation logic, validation pitfalls, and conceptual distinctions that matter when machine-learning methods are applied to economic data.

## How This Book Relates to Other Resources {.unnumbered}

Several good resources cover adjacent territory, and it helps to say briefly how this book fits in.

[*Machine Learning for Factor Investing*](https://www.mlfactor.com/) by Coqueret and Guida is a practitioner-oriented guide to ML in asset pricing, with a focus on return prediction and portfolio formation. Readers looking for a complement to this book, especially on financial applications, will find it useful. This book differs mainly in emphasis: it assumes a graduate econometrics background and spends more time on dependence, forecast evaluation, and distributional uncertainty. It also covers topics such as conformal prediction, foundation models for economic text, and distributional neural networks that fall outside the asset pricing focus of that book.

Standard ML textbooks such as *An Introduction to Statistical Learning* or *Hands-On Machine Learning* are excellent introductions for readers without a prior statistics or econometrics background. This book is not a replacement for them; it is intended for readers who already have that background and want to connect ML methods to the econometric settings they work in.

## Feedback and Updates {.unnumbered}

This book is an ongoing project. If you spot a typo, find an unclear explanation, or have suggestions for additional material, please open an issue or get in touch through the repository: [github.com/onnokleen/mlmetrics](https://github.com/onnokleen/mlmetrics).

The source for the book lives in the same repository, and the rendered website is updated as the material evolves.

## Author and License {.unnumbered}

[**Onno Kleen**](https://onnokleen.com/) is an Assistant Professor of Econometrics at the [Department of Econometrics at Erasmus University Rotterdam](https://www.eur.nl/en/ese/department-econometrics/) and a fellow at the [Tinbergen Institute](https://tinbergen.nl).

This book is licensed under [Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International CC BY-NC-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/).
